codeReview.summary: The feature successfully adds blocked-secret state tracking, keystore invalidation handling, and repair messaging, but the host repair flow can silently relink a host to the wrong identity once blocked identities are filtered out.

issues.description: When a saved host's linked identity is blocked or otherwise absent from `availableIdentities`, the editor defaults `selectedIdentityId` to `availableIdentities.firstOrNull()?.id`. Because this feature also filters host options to `identities.filter { it.isAuthenticationReady }`, opening a repair/edit flow for a blocked host can preselect an unrelated healthy identity and silently relink the host on the next save without an explicit user choice. Recovery is supposed to be explicit, so the blocked link should remain unselected until the user actively picks a replacement or repairs the original identity.

sharedStateObservations.observation: The shared architecture docs do not record the new secret-lifecycle convention that identities can be `MISSING`, `AVAILABLE`, or `BLOCKED`, nor that host/auth surfaces must gate on `isAuthenticationReady` rather than `hasSecret`. Future workers touching SSH/auth flows would have to rediscover this behavior from code.
sharedStateObservations.evidence: `core/domain/src/main/kotlin/io/github/jtsang4/aterm/core/domain/model/Identity.kt:11-15,45-51` defines the new state machine and readiness rule, while `.factory/library/architecture.md:29-38,64-70,93-100` still describes the security and host/identity boundaries without documenting blocked-secret repair semantics.

summary: Fail. I reviewed the feature handoff, transcript skeleton, and commit `892b3971b84c8083ecc6762e37f3b1c3729563a8`. The keystore recovery work is mostly sound, but there is one blocking correctness issue: repairing/editing a host with a blocked identity can auto-select a different identity and silently change the host link on save.
